l1: Question 1 — When did the NTFS corruption issue first appear?
Question 2 — Is the corruption isolated to one volume or multiple?
Question 3 — Are users reporting file access errors or application crashes?
Question 4 — Has the server experienced unexpected shutdowns or power loss?
Question 5 — Was the volume recently resized, migrated, or restored?
Question 6 — Are permissions or ACLs failing on affected files?
Question 7 — Is the issue impacting production workloads or test environments?
Question 8 — Are shadow copies or checkpoints failing on the volume?
l2: Advanced question 1 — Are there Event Viewer logs showing NTFS errors (Event ID 55, 98)?
Advanced question 2 — Is the corruption linked to underlying disk or RAID issues?
Advanced question 3 — Is deduplication or compression enabled on the volume?
Advanced question 4 — Is the volume part of clustered storage?
Advanced question 5 — Has BitLocker or encryption been applied?
Advanced question 6 — Is corruption reproducible after restoring from backup?
Advanced question 7 — Are system files impacted by corruption?
Advanced question 8 — Are applications reporting NTFS journal errors?

l1: Command 1 — Run chkdsk /scan on NTFS volumes
Command 2 — Check Event Viewer → System log for NTFS errors
Command 3 — Run PowerShell Get-Volume to confirm health status
Command 4 — Verify disk health with Get-Disk
Command 5 — Attempt file access to confirm corruption symptoms
Command 6 — Run fsutil fsinfo volumeinfo to check filesystem details
Command 7 — Review Disk Management for volume state
Command 8 — Check permissions with icacls on affected files
l2: Advanced command 1 — Run chkdsk /f /r for full NTFS repair
Advanced command 2 — Use PowerShell Repair-Volume -DriveLetter X -OfflineScanAndFix
Advanced command 3 — Query cluster logs if volume is clustered
Advanced command 4 — Run Get-StoragePool and Get-VirtualDisk for underlying storage health
Advanced command 5 — Use SFC /scannow if system files are impacted
Advanced command 6 — Run DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth for OS-level corruption
Advanced command 7 — Review deduplication logs if enabled
Advanced command 8 — Run fsutil resource setautoreset true C:\ to reset NTFS journal

l1: Step 1 — Restart the server and rerun chkdsk /scan
Step 2 — Run Repair-Volume for minor NTFS issues
Step 3 — Restore affected files from backup
Step 4 — Verify and reset file permissions with icacls
Step 5 — Ensure sufficient free disk space for repair operations
Step 6 — Disable deduplication temporarily if causing corruption
Step 7 — Run SFC /scannow for system file integrity
Step 8 — Validate disk health with vendor diagnostic tools
l2: Advanced fix 1 — Run chkdsk /f /r to repair NTFS corruption
Advanced fix 2 — Use Repair-Volume -OfflineScanAndFix for deeper repair
Advanced fix 3 — Rebuild RAID array if underlying disk issues detected
Advanced fix 4 — Restore volume from snapshot or checkpoint
Advanced fix 5 — Recreate corrupted volume and restore data from backup
Advanced fix 6 — Update storage controller firmware and drivers
Advanced fix 7 — Escalate to Microsoft support if NTFS journal corruption persists
Advanced fix 8 — Perform cluster validation if clustered storage is impacted

l1: Collection 1 — Export Event Viewer logs from System → NTFS errors
Collection 2 — Run Get-Volume and export results
Collection 3 — Execute chkdsk /scan output and save
Collection 4 — Capture screenshot of Disk Management showing affected volumes
Collection 5 — Collect OS build and patch level
Collection 6 — Export fsutil fsinfo volumeinfo output
Collection 7 — Gather icacls permissions output for affected files
Collection 8 — Save SMART data from Get-PhysicalDisk
l2: Advanced collection 1 — Export chkdsk /f /r detailed logs
Advanced collection 2 — Collect Repair-Volume -OfflineScanAndFix results
Advanced collection 3 — Gather cluster logs if volume is clustered
Advanced collection 4 — Collect RAID controller logs and config dump
Advanced collection 5 — Export deduplication logs if enabled
Advanced collection 6 — Gather DISM and SFC logs for OS-level corruption
Advanced collection 7 — Collect firmware/driver versions for storage controllers
Advanced collection 8 — Export fsutil resource logs for NTFS journal

steps.expect: Volume shows degraded or unhealthy state

steps.action: Restore files from backup if unrecoverable
